1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-176 Lane K file-skeleton)
36	
37	This file is the **iter-176 Lane K** file-skeleton (a re-dispatch of the
38	iter-175 Lane K casualty that died to the Anthropic session-limit reset
39	window before the file was ever created). Each of the five pinned
40	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
41	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex`) with a
42	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work after the sibling chapters
43	`RR.1`'s `RationalMap.order` body lands (iter-176 Lane D target) and the
44	`RR.2` cohomology-LES API stabilises.
45	
46	The 5 pinned declarations are:
47	
48	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
49	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
50	   curve `C / k̄`.
51	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff`
52	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the
53	   Riemann–Roch space
54	   `L([P]) = {f ∈ K(C)^× | div(f) + [P] ≥ 0} ∪ {0}`,
55	   expressed as an `Iff`-style characterisation of the order conditions
56	   `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
57	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero`
58	   — the cohomological vanishing `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a smooth proper
59	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, via the long
60	   exact sequence of the closed-point short exact sequence
61	   `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`.
62	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
63	   — the dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus `0`,
64	   specialising the χ-identity `RR.2` to `D = [P]` and consuming the
65	   `H¹`-vanishing of pin 3.
66	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
67	   — the corollary: a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` regular
68	   on `C \ {P}` with at most a simple pole at `P`, obtained as a lift of
69	   any non-zero element of the quotient
70	   `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) / 𝓀̄ · 1`.
71	
72	## Notation reminders
73	
74	The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` is realised as a
75	`Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`,
76	the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf category used by the project's
77	`Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (cf. `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`).
78	Its `H⁰` and `H¹` are computed via `Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 0` and
79	`Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 1`, both of which carry a canonical `Module k̄`
80	instance.

102	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
103	
104	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
105	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
106	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
107	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
108	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
109	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
110	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
111	
112	namespace Scheme
113	
114	/-- **The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
115	curve `C / k̄`** (Hartshorne II §6, p. 144, Proposition 6.13(a)).
116	
117	The invertible sheaf cut out (in the dual / `𝒦_C`-subsheaf packaging of
118	Hartshorne `ℒ(D)`) by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on the complement,
119	where `f_P ∈ 𝔪_P ∖ 𝔪_P²` is any uniformiser of the DVR `𝒪_{C,P}`. The
120	result is independent of the choice of uniformiser (two uniformisers
121	differ by a unit) and is an invertible `𝒪_C`-module of rank one.
122	
123	The signature returns a `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
124	(ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`: the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf
125	carrier used by the project's `Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (so
126	that `H⁰` and `H¹` of `𝒪_C(P)` are accessible via
127	`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 0/1`).
128	
129	iter-177+ body: glue the inverse-of-ideal-sheaf construction. Concretely,
130	build the ideal sheaf `ℐ_P ⊆ 𝒪_C` of the closed point `P` (locally
131	generated by the uniformiser `f_P` near `P`, equal to `𝒪_C` on the
132	complement) via Mathlib's
133	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData.idealOfPoint`, then take its
134	`𝒪_C`-linear dual `ℋom_{𝒪_C}(ℐ_P, 𝒪_C) ≅ ℐ_P⁻¹`, and finally forget
135	the `O_C`-structure into the `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf via the
136	`HModule` adjunction's left adjoint.
137	
138	Blueprint reference: `def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint`
139	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 144 + Proposition 6.13(a); Stacks tag 01X0). -/
140	noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint
141	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
142	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
143	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
144	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
145	    (_P : C.left) (_hP : IsClosed ({_P} : Set C.left)) :
146	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
147	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=

173	/-! ## §2. Global sections as the Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`
174	
175	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
176	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
177	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
178	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
179	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/
180	
181	/-- **Forward direction of `globalSections_iff` (Hartshorne II.7.7(b)).**
182	
183	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`
184	and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`, the rational function `f` lifts to a global section
185	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
186	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
187	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
188	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
189	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
190	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
191	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
192	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`
193	on any affine open `U ⊆ C ∖ {P}`).

269	pole at `P`** (Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7, p. 157).
270	
271	For a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×`, the following are
272	equivalent:
273	
274	* there exists a global section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under

279	  `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (where the latter is read off the prime divisor
280	  `⟨P, h⟩` with `h : Order.coheight P = 1` the codimension-one witness
281	  automatic for a closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme).

283	The iff is the substantive content of Hartshorne's Proposition 7.7(b) /
284	its proof, specialised to `D_0 = [P]`.

320	/-! ## §3. Cohomological vanishing in genus zero
321	
322	Specialise Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's inductive step at `D = 0`. The
323	standard short exact sequence
324	`0 → 𝒪_C(−[P]) → 𝒪_C → k(P) → 0` (Hartshorne II.6.18: the ideal sheaf
325	of the locally principal closed subscheme `P` is `𝒪_C(−[P])`; the
326	quotient is the skyscraper `k(P) ≅ k̄` at `P`) tensored by the locally
327	free rank-`1` sheaf `𝒪_C([P])` (left rigid, so preserves exactness and
328	leaves the skyscraper invariant) becomes
329	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0` in `Coh(C)`. The associated long exact
330	sequence of sheaf cohomology, combined with `H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0`
331	(genus-`0` hypothesis: `g(C) = dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`) and
332	`H¹(C, k(P)) = 0` (skyscraper / flasque), kills `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))`. -/
333	
334	/-- **Vanishing of `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))` on a smooth proper geometrically
335	irreducible curve of genus `0`** (Hartshorne IV §1 p. 296, the
336	inductive step of Theorem 1.3 specialised to `D = 0`).
337	
338	Concretely, the finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector space
339	`Scheme.HModule kbar (lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP) 1` has dimension
340	`0`, i.e. is the trivial vector space.
341	
342	iter-177+ body: assemble the closed-point short exact sequence
343	`0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`, feed it to the long exact sequence of
344	`Module k̄`-flavoured cohomology (the project's
345	`Scheme.HModule k̄`-bridge inherits the LES by forget-functor
346	naturality from
347	`CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence_exact`), substitute
348	`H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (the genus-`0` hypothesis, unfolding

350	flasque cohomology, Hartshorne III.2.5), and collapse the segment to
351	`0 → H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) → 0`.
352	
353	Blueprint reference: `lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero`
354	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 296). -/

363	
364	Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity

366	(`Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`) to
367	`D = [P]`. Since `deg([P]) = 1` (every closed point contributes degree
368	`1` over `k̄`) and `g(C) = 0`, this gives `χ(𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. Unfolding
369	`χ` as `dim H⁰ − dim H¹` and substituting the `H¹`-vanishing of §3
370	yields `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. -/
371

397	/-! ## §5. A non-constant rational function with at most a simple pole at `P`
398	
399	The two-dimensionality of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` and the one-dimensional
400	constant subspace `k̄ · 1` give a non-zero quotient `H⁰/k̄`. Any lift
401	of a non-zero element of the quotient is, under the identification of
402	`globalSections_iff`, a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with
403	the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
404	This is the seed of `RR.4` (the morphism `C → ℙ¹` produced by
405	`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` from the basis `(1, f)`). -/
406	
407	/-- **Existence of a non-constant rational function regular on `C ∖ {P}`
408	with at most a simple pole at `P`** (Hartshorne IV §1 Exercise 1.1,
409	p. 297, the genus-`0` specialisation).
410	
411	Concretely, there exists `f ∈ K(C)` such that:
412	
413	* `f ≠ 0`;
414	* `f ∉ k̄` (i.e. `f` is non-constant — for instance, it does not lie
415	  in the image of the structural inclusion of constants);
416	* `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose
417	  generic point is not `P`;
418	* `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
419	
420	iter-177+ body: use `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero` to get
421	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. The image of `1 ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) ≅ k̄`
422	under the structural inclusion `𝒪_C ↪ 𝒪_C(P)` spans a one-dimensional
423	subspace of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`; choose any section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`
424	not in this constant subspace (non-empty because `dim H⁰ = 2 > 1`),
425	then take `f := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s` and
426	verify the four bullets via `globalSections_iff` applied to `f`
427	(the forward direction supplies the order conditions from the existence
428	witness `⟨s, rfl⟩`). The chosen `f` is non-constant because `s` is not
429	in the constant subspace and `toFunctionField` is `k̄`-linear and
430	injective on global sections.

432	The principal-divisor-non-zero formulation `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
433	f hf ≠ 0` follows from non-constancy plus the fact that constant

435	⇒ `f` constant — uses the integrality of `C` and is the Stacks 02P0
436	"functions with no zeros and poles are constant" type statement).

438	Blueprint reference:

441	(Hartshorne IV.1 Exercise 1.1, p. 297). -/

446	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)

71	`x` is a regular element. Two cases:

73	- `depth M = d+1`: there's a regular element `x`, so `depth (M/xM) = d`,
74	  and the LES of `Ext^*(κ, ·)` gives the recursive identity.
75	
76	ETA ~100-150 LOC. Reuse iter-182's `ext_vanish_of_natCast_lt_depth`
77	helper (Tier-2 modulo `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` itself — so this

3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	
104	\leanok
105	[Line bundle of a closed point]
106	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
108	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
109	        def:order_at_point}
110	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
111	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
112	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
114	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
115	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
116	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
117	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
118	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
119	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
120	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
121	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
122	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
123	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
124	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
125	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
126	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
127	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
128	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
129	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
130	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
131	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
132	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
133	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
134	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
135	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
136	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
137	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
138	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
139	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
140	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
141	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
142	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
143	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
144	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
145	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
146	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
147	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
148	
149	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
150	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
151	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
152	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
153	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
154	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
155	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
156	  \[
157	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
158	    \mathcal O_C)
159	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
160	  \]
161	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
162	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
163	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
164	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
165	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
166	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
167	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
168	
169	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
171	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
172	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
173	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
174	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
175	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
176	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
177	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
178	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
179	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
180	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
181	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
182	\end{definition}
183

187	[Inclusion of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ into the function field]

192	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$; the
193	  % canonical inclusion $\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K$ is part
194	  % of the construction) (read from
195	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
197	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
198	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
199	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
200	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
201	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
202	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call

219	  function on $C$ obtained by composing $s$ with the inclusion of

221	  injective (the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset

238	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	
243	\leanok
244	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
245	controlled pole]
246	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
248	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
249	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField,
250	        def:order_at_point,
251	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
252	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
253	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
254	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
255	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
256	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
257	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
258	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
259	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
260	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
261	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
262	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
263	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
264	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
265	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
266	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
267	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
268	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
269	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
270	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
271	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
272	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
273	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
274	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
275	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
276	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
277	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
278	  Riemann--Roch space
279	  \[
280	    L([P]) \;:=\;
281	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
282	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
283	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
284	  \]
285	  Equivalently, the order conditions
286	  \[
287	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
288	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
289	    \qquad
290	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
291	  \]
292	  on $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ are equivalent to the existence of a global
293	  section $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ whose image $\iota(s)$ under
294	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
295	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)$ equals $f$ (the iff binds $s$ to $f$
296	  explicitly — vacuous-in-$f$ readings such as ``some nonzero global
297	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
298	
299	  Under this identification, the inclusion
300	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
301	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
302	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
303	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{proof}
307	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
308	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
309	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
310	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
311	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
312	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
313	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
314	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
315	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
316	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
317	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
318	  and only if:
319	
320	  \begin{itemize}
321	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
322	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
323	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
324	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
325	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
326	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
327	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
328	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
329	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
330	  \end{itemize}
331	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
332	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
333	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
334	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
335	  zero global section.
336	
337	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
338	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
339	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
340	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
341	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
342	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
343	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
344	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
345	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
346	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
347	\end{proof}

349	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
350	
351	\begin{lemma}
352	
353	
354	\leanok
355	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
356	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
358	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
359	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
360	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
361	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
362	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
363	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
364	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
365	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
366	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
367	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
368	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
369	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
370	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
371	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
372	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
373	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
374	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
375	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
376	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
377	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
378	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
379	  \[
380	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
381	  \]
382	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
383	\end{lemma}
384	
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
387	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
388	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
389	  exact sequence
390	  \[
391	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
392	  \]
393	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
394	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
395	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
396	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
397	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
398	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
399	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
400	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
401	  \[
402	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
403	  \]
404	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
405	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
406	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
407	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
408	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
409	  \begin{multline*}
410	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
411	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
412	  \end{multline*}
413	  Two ingredients close the proof:
414	
415	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
416	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
417	  \[
418	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
419	  \]
420	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
421	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
422	
423	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
424	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
425	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
426	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
427	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
428	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
429	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
430	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
431	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
432	
433	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
434	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
435	  \[
436	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
437	  \]
438	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
439	
440	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
441	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
442	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
443	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
444	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
445	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
446	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
447	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
448	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
449	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
450	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
451	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
452	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
453	  complex degenerates).
454	\end{proof}
455	
456	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
457	
458	\begin{theorem}
459	
460	
461	\leanok
462	[$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
463	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
464	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
465	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
466	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
467	        def:eulerChar_curve,
468	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
469	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
470	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
471	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with $D = [P]$: $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
472	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
473	  % $H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0$ (read from
474	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
475	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
476	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
477	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
478	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
479	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
480	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
481	  % is the Euler characteristic
482	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
483	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
484	  $\chi$-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
485	  genus-$0$ specialisation chain).}
486	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
487	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
488	  \[
489	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
490	  \]
491	\end{theorem}
492	
493	\begin{proof}
494	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
495	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
496	        def:eulerChar_curve,
497	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
498	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
499	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
500	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2} to
501	  the divisor $D = [P]$ of \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The
502	  degree of $[P]$ is $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
503	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
504	  genus hypothesis $g(C) = 0$ (\Cref{def:genus}) gives
505	  \[
506	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
507	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
508	  \]
509	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} as a two-term Euler
510	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
511	  \[
512	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
513	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
514	    \;=\; 2.
515	  \]
516	  By
517	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}, the second
518	  term vanishes:
519	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$. Substituting yields
520	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
523	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
524	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} computes
525	  the dimension of $H^0$ in one stroke without invoking
526	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
527	  \[
528	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
529	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
530	  \]
531	  together with $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$
532	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: $H^0$ of the structure sheaf on a proper
533	  geometrically irreducible variety over $\bar k$ is $\bar k$),
534	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ (skyscraper at a $\bar k$-rational
535	  point), and $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ (genus-$0$), the connecting
536	  map $H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ has zero codomain and the
537	  segment yields the short exact sequence
538	  \[
539	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
540	  \]
541	  of $\bar k$-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
542	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
543	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
544	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
545	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
546	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
547	\end{proof}

549	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
550	
551	\begin{corollary}
552	
553	
554	\leanok
555	[A genus-$0$ curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
556	single simple pole]
557	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
558	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
559	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
560	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
562	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
563	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
564	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
565	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
566	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
567	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
568	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
569	  % point on a genus-$0$ curve, as a direct consequence of the
570	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
571	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let $X$ be a curve, and let $P \in X$ be a
573	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
574	  % K(X)$, which is regular everywhere except at $P$."
575	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
576	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
577	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
578	  there exists a non-constant rational function $f \in K(C)$ such that:
579	  \begin{itemize}
580	    \item $f$ is regular on $C \setminus \{P\}$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
581	      for every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$;
582	    \item $f$ has at most a simple pole at $P$, i.e.\
583	      $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$;
584	    \item $f \notin \bar k$, equivalently
585	      $\div(f) \neq 0 \in \Div(C)$.
586	  \end{itemize}
587	  In particular, the pair $(1, f)$ spans
588	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
589	\end{corollary}
590	
591	\begin{proof}
592	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
593	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
594	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
595	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
596	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
597	  By
598	  \Cref{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero},
599	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$. The constant subspace
600	  $\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, obtained from the
601	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
602	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
603	  $f = 1$, with $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, under the
604	  identification of
605	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}), is a one-dimensional
606	  $\bar k$-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension $2$,
607	  the quotient $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1$ is a
608	  non-zero one-dimensional $\bar k$-vector space; any lift to
609	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of a non-zero element of the quotient is
610	  an element of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1$.
611	
612	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
613	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
614	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} the section
615	  $s$ corresponds to a rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$
616	  satisfying $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, equivalently $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
617	  for every $Q \neq P$ and $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$. The choice
618	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ means $f$ is not the image of a constant,
619	  so $f \notin \bar k$ (every non-zero element of
620	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k$ corresponds, under the
621	  identification, to a constant element of $K(C)$). The three bullets
622	  of the statement are now immediate.
623	
624	  Spanning. Linear independence of $1$ and $f$ over $\bar k$ in
625	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ is the same condition as
626	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ above; combined with the dimension count
627	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, the pair $(1, f)$ is a
628	  $\bar k$-basis of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$.
629	
630	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
631	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
632	  returns an element $f$ of the project's function-field carrier
633	  $K(C)$ together with proofs of regularity on $C \setminus \{P\}$ and
634	  the simple-pole bound at $P$, packaged in the same shape as
635	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}'s output. The
636	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
637	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
638	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
639	\end{proof}
640	
641	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H$^1$-vanishing input}
642	
643	The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve
644	(\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}) is the
645	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
646	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
647	elsewhere:
648	
649	\begin{itemize}
650	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of $\Module \bar k$-flavoured sheaf
651	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
652	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
653	    $\Ext^*$-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
654	    project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured pipeline
655	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
656	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
657	    consumed by \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of
658	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
659	    obligation.
660	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
661	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
662	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
663	    (\Cref{def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC} and surrounding
664	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
665	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
666	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
667	    $\chi(k(P)) = 1$, which is the combined statement
668	    $\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ and $\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$,
669	    so the vanishing $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ is already a consumer
670	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
671	\end{itemize}
672	
673	Consequently the H$^1$-vanishing
674	\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} introduces no
675	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
676	path. The genus-$0$ specialisation should therefore close at the
677	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
678	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.
679	
680	% NOTE (iter-174 plan-agent flag). The general-$g$ vanishing statement
681	% ``$H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0$ whenever $\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2$''
682	% (Hartshorne~IV.1, the Riemann--Roch consequence read covariantly) is
683	% \emph{not} a deliverable of this chapter and is not required for the
684	% genus-$0$ critical path: the genus-$0$ argument closes via the long
685	% exact sequence at $D = 0$ without ever needing the general bound. The
686	% general bound is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement and is queued for
687	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter together with the dualizing sheaf
688	% $\omega_C$ and the canonical-divisor identification $K_C \sim -2[P]$
689	% on $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne~II.8.20.1).
690	
691	\section{Out of scope}
692	
693	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.3}
694	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for
695	follow-up iterations:
696	
697	\begin{itemize}
698	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$
699	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
700	    \Div(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
701	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when $D \sim D'$, are
702	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
703	    the specialisation $\mathcal O_C(D)$ at $D = [P]$ a prime divisor,
704	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-$0$ classification
705	    chain. The general $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is queued for the
706	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-$g$
707	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
708	  \item The general-$g$ statement
709	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
710	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
711	    consequence of
712	    \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2},
713	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
714	    genus-$0$ specialisation $g = 0$ on which
715	    \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} is
716	    available.
717	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
718	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
719	    canonical divisor $K_C = c_1(\omega_C)$ are \emph{not} used at any
720	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
721	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
722	    $D = 0$ (\Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero})
723	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
724	  \item The morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ produced by applying
725	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis $(1, f)$ of
726	    \Cref{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero},
727	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
728	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
729	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
730	    chapter supplies the basis $(1, f)$ and stops.
731	  \item The general-$g$ vanishing
732	    $H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0$ for $\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2$ is
733	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
734	    genus-$0$ critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
735	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
736	  \item The interpretation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as the space
737	    of sections of a complete linear system $|[P]|$ in
738	    projective-space form (Hartshorne~II.7.7) is gestured at by
739	    \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} but the
740	    projective-space structure on the underlying set is not separately
741	    formalised; the chapter consumes only the $\bar k$-vector space
742	    structure on $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$.
743	\end{itemize}
